completeness.rationale: Six of seven expected items were captured: linear memory growth pattern, v2.3 upgrade timing (~1 week ago), 200 active users, consistent day-to-day pattern, daily restart as workaround, and the v2.3 changelog mention of notification system changes. The missing item is server specs (8GB RAM VM, Ubuntu 22.04), which was never asked about. The triage also surfaced additional useful information not in the expected list, such as the ~25 requests/user/hour rate and that users actively use real-time notification features.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis closely matches the actual cause. The agent identified the v2.3 upgrade as the trigger, the real-time notification system as the likely source, and specifically called out 'event listeners or notification subscription objects that are created but never cleaned up' — which is precisely the actual mechanism. This satisfies both acceptable diagnostic path #1 (v2.3 + notification system) and approaches the canonical root cause. No meaningful inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked the right questions to establish the per-request leak pattern and identify the v2.3 trigger. It followed up appropriately on the notification system angle. The main gap is that server hardware and OS details (8GB RAM, Ubuntu 22.04) were never requested, and the runtime/language stack was noted as an information gap rather than asked about. These are minor omissions given that the core diagnostic picture was clear.

economy.rationale: Three turns, each well-targeted and non-redundant. Turn 1 established the usage pattern to characterize the leak. Turn 2 discovered the version upgrade trigger. Turn 3 probed the notification mechanism and proposed a mitigation test. No question could be reasonably cut or combined without losing diagnostic value.

question_quality.rationale: The questions demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning. Turn 1's focus on whether memory growth tracked specific workflows vs. overall volume was a clever way to distinguish a per-feature leak from a per-request one. Turn 2's open-ended 'did anything change' question efficiently surfaced the v2.3 upgrade and, critically, the reporter's own changelog observation about notifications — information the reporter had but didn't volunteer in the original report. Turn 3's specific probe about WebSocket connection counts and configuration options showed mechanistic thinking about the leak.

actionability.rationale: The summary gives a developer an immediate starting point: diff the notification subsystem between v2.2 and v2.3, look for per-request listener or connection registration without corresponding cleanup, run a heap dump after several hours. Concrete reproduction steps with expected memory growth rates (~400-500MB/hour) and a proposed test case with pass/fail criteria are included. A developer could begin code review or profiling directly from this summary.

notable_strengths: Turn 1's framing around whether memory growth tracks specific workflows vs. overall request volume was a sophisticated diagnostic move that immediately characterized the leak as per-request rather than per-feature
Turn 2's open-ended change question efficiently surfaced the v2.3 upgrade and the reporter's own changelog observation — information present but not volunteered in the original report
The final summary is unusually actionable: it includes specific investigation angles, concrete reproduction steps with quantified expected metrics, and a well-scoped proposed test case
notable_weaknesses: Server hardware specs (8GB RAM VM) and OS (Ubuntu 22.04) were never asked about and are absent from the summary — minor but part of the expected extracts
The server-side runtime/language was correctly flagged as an information gap but could have been asked directly, since it would meaningfully affect which profiling tools to recommend
most_insightful_question: Turn 2: 'did anything change around the time it began — a version upgrade, a configuration change, adding a new integration or plugin' — this open-ended framing drew out the v2.3 upgrade and the reporter's own unprompted connection to the changelog's notification system changes, which became the central diagnostic lead.
missed_opportunities: Ask about server hardware specs (RAM, OS) to complete the environment picture and inform mitigation options (e.g., increasing swap as a short-term workaround)
Ask about the server-side language or runtime, which would allow recommending specific profiling tools (e.g., Go pprof, Node.js heap snapshots, JVM heap dumps) rather than leaving this as a gap
